content.text: A launch checklist for an agentic AI system is a governance artifact, not a to-do list. A to-do list is informally shared and collectively owned, which in practice means it is nobody's responsibility. A governance artifact has a named owner for every item — someone whose professional accountability is attached to the task's completion. The moment a checklist item lacks a named owner, it becomes the task that everyone assumes someone else is handling, and it will not be done when it matters. This sub-subtopic covers the mechanics of assigning, tracking, and enforcing task ownership across the full scope of an AI feature launch.
What It Is

Launch readiness task ownership is the practice of assigning every item on the launch checklist to a single Directly Responsible Individual (DRI) — one person who is accountable for either completing the task or escalating a blocker. The DRI model, popularized by Apple and widely adopted in tech organizations, is explicit: for every task, one name appears in the Owner field. Not a team name, not a role, not two people sharing responsibility. One individual.

For an agentic AI launch, the checklist spans at least seven categories: infrastructure and deployment, security and compliance, data and ML, AI-specific readiness (model card, hallucination guard, rate limits), operations and observability, legal and communications, and product and business readiness. Each category may have 5–15 individual tasks. Without explicit DRI assignment, it is common for a launch to arrive with several of these tasks incomplete because each team assumed another team was handling the overlap.
Why It Matters

Post-launch incident retrospectives for AI systems consistently surface the same root cause: a critical task had no named owner, or two people believed they shared ownership and each assumed the other was doing the work. The rollback plan wasn't written because both SRE and engineering thought the other team owned it. The rate-limit configuration wasn't tested because it sat in a gray zone between platform engineering and the AI application team.

Explicit ownership assignment does not prevent every mistake, but it eliminates the specific class of failures caused by diffusion of responsibility. When every task has one name attached to it, the launch lead has a complete accountability map: they can see at a glance which items are done, which are in progress, which are blocked, and who to escalate to for each blocked item. Without that map, launch readiness reviews become exercises in collective uncertainty.
Benefits
Eliminates diffusion of responsibility.
When one person owns a task, the social dynamic shifts from "someone will handle this" to "I will handle this."
Enables precise escalation.

A launch lead who knows that task X is owned by Alice can escalate directly and efficiently. Without ownership, escalation requires discovering who should own the task before the conversation about its status can begin.
Creates a launch readiness signal.
When every task has a status, the percentage of tasks completed is a meaningful launch readiness metric that can be tracked over time and compared across launches.
Distributes work correctly.
Explicit ownership assignment exposes imbalances — one person owns 30 tasks while another owns 2 — that would otherwise remain invisible until someone is overwhelmed.
Enables parallel progress.
Tasks with independent owners can be executed simultaneously rather than sequentially, compressing the launch preparation timeline.
Improves retrospectives.
A record of who owned what task, what the status was at launch, and what happened post-launch is essential for identifying process improvements in future launches.
Trade-offs
Assignment overhead.
Assigning owners requires a meeting or process where ownership is explicitly negotiated. For teams new to this practice, this conversation can take 1–2 hours for a full launch checklist.
Single-point-of-failure risk.
A DRI who becomes unavailable (illness, vacation, departure) leaves their tasks without an owner unless backup DRI assignments are also recorded.
Ownership without expertise.

Assigning a task to someone who does not have the expertise to complete it creates the appearance of ownership without the substance. Ownership must be accompanied by the resources and access needed to do the work.
Gaming the status.
Without verification, owners may mark tasks complete before they are genuinely done to avoid escalation. Launch readiness reviews must include spot-checks of completed items.
How It Works

Launch readiness task ownership operates through a structured assignment process followed by a tracking cadence. The assignment process begins with a launch kickoff meeting, typically 4–6 weeks before the target launch date. The launch lead presents the checklist and, for each task, asks for a volunteer DRI or assigns one based on team responsibility. The assignment is recorded in the tracking system immediately — not after the meeting, not in a separate document.

After assignment, the tracking system is the source of truth. The launch lead runs a weekly launch readiness review where each DRI reports the current status of their tasks. Blocked tasks are escalated immediately. The launch lead does not wait for the DRI to escalate; they actively ask about the status of high-risk tasks in every review.

For large launches with many tasks, the launch lead assigns category leads — one person responsible for a category of tasks (e.g., the ML lead owns all AI-specific readiness tasks and reports the category's status to the launch lead). The category lead owns the sub-tasks within their category and manages within-category escalations. The launch lead receives a category-level status summary, not a task-level one.
RACI Applied to Launch Tasks

RACI is a framework for clarifying roles on any task: Responsible (the person doing the work), Accountable (the person who is ultimately answerable for the outcome), Consulted (people whose input is needed), and Informed (people who should be notified of the outcome).

In the DRI model, Responsible and Accountable are collapsed into the single DRI. This simplification works well for operational tasks where the goal is execution speed. For higher-stakes decisions embedded in the launch checklist — "approve the rollout percentage," "sign off on the model card" — the full RACI distinction is valuable: one person is Responsible for compiling the review package, a different (usually more senior) person is Accountable for approving it.
Responsible (R):
The person doing the work. For "write the on-call runbook," the responsible party is the SRE who actually writes it.
Accountable (A):
The person who is answerable if the task is not done correctly or on time. Only one person can be accountable per task. For "write the on-call runbook," the SRE manager may be accountable.
Consulted (C):
People whose expertise is needed. For the on-call runbook, the engineering team that built the feature is consulted because they know the failure modes.
Informed (I):
People who should know the task is done. For the on-call runbook, the on-call team, the launch lead, and product management are informed when it is complete.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Launch Readiness RACI table: Task categories (Infra/Deploy, Model/ML, Security, Legal/Comms) mapped to DRI owners, Accountable roles, Consulted and Informed parties. Status legend: Done, In Progress, Blocked, Not Started, Escalated. Footer: one DRI per task is the core rule.

content.text: The diagram shows the RACI structure applied to four representative task categories. Each category has a designated DRI (the Responsible column), a senior role who is Accountable, domain experts who are Consulted, and stakeholders who are Informed. The status legend at the bottom reflects the five states a task can be in during the launch preparation period.
Categories of Launch Tasks and Their Owners
Infrastructure and Deployment

Infrastructure tasks include provisioning compute resources (GPU nodes for inference, load balancers, CDN configuration), configuring the deployment pipeline, validating rollback mechanisms, and confirming that the deployment is repeatable and automated. The natural DRI for these tasks is the platform engineering or SRE lead for the service. AI-specific infrastructure tasks — sizing GPU node pools for LLM inference workloads, configuring token rate-limit guards at the API gateway, validating cold-start latency for model loading — require a DRI who understands both the infrastructure layer and the AI system's resource characteristics.
Security and Compliance

Security tasks include penetration testing, dependency vulnerability scanning, secrets rotation, data flow review, and prompt injection defense validation. The DRI for security tasks should be a security engineer, not a member of the feature engineering team (who may lack the adversarial mindset needed for effective security review). For AI-specific security tasks — prompt injection resistance testing, output filter validation, tool-call scope enforcement — the DRI needs both security expertise and AI system knowledge. If a single person with both skill sets is not available, assign two DRIs: one for the task and one as the AI expert consultant.
Data and ML Readiness

Data and ML tasks include completing the evaluation suite and reviewing results, writing and reviewing the model card, completing the bias audit, validating data pipeline correctness (for systems that retrieve data as part of tool use), and confirming that model version is pinned correctly in the deployment configuration. The DRI for these tasks is typically the ML engineer or data scientist who built or fine-tuned the model. Tasks requiring external review (bias audit, model card sign-off) should have the external reviewer listed as the DRI, with the internal ML engineer as the accountable backup.
AI-Specific Readiness Tasks

Beyond the standard categories, agentic AI launches have a class of tasks specific to the AI components that lack natural ownership in most engineering organizations. These tasks require explicit DRI assignment because they sit at the intersection of multiple teams.
Hallucination guard configuration.
Configuring output filters or confidence thresholds that flag or suppress potentially incorrect outputs. DRI: ML engineer.
Rate-limit configuration.

Setting per-user and per-system token rate limits to control cost and prevent denial-of-service through expensive LLM calls. DRI: platform/SRE lead, with ML input on expected token consumption per request.
Context window limit validation.
Confirming that the system handles gracefully the cases where accumulated context exceeds the model's context window. DRI: feature engineering lead.
Model card completion and sign-off.
Completing the standardized model card documenting the model's intended use, known limitations, evaluation results, and bias audit findings. DRI: ML lead, with sign-off by legal and product.
AI disclosure implementation.
Implementing the user-facing disclosure that responses are AI-generated. DRI: frontend engineer, with legal review of disclosure text.
Operations and Observability

Operational readiness tasks include completing on-call runbooks, configuring alerting rules and SLO burn-rate alerts, verifying that structured logging is in place and that logs are being indexed correctly in the log management system, and confirming that dashboards are populated and reviewed by the on-call team. The DRI for these tasks is the SRE or on-call engineer, not the feature engineer. A runbook written by the engineer who built the system but never reviewed by the person who will be on-call for it is incomplete — the on-call engineer must confirm they can understand and execute it.
When to Use It

Apply DRI assignment to every launch, regardless of scale. Even a small AI feature with five launch tasks benefits from explicit ownership — the overhead is minimal and the accountability benefit is material.
For large launches with 50+ tasks, use a category lead model: one DRI per category, with sub-task ownership tracked within each category.
Assign DRIs at the launch kickoff meeting, not in a follow-up email. Synchronous assignment is more likely to result in genuine acceptance of responsibility.
Record backup DRIs for every task on the critical path. If the primary DRI is unavailable, the backup should be able to take over without a gap.
How to Implement

# launch_tasks.yaml — launch checklist with DRI ownership for an agentic AI feature
# Use with the task_status_reporter.py script to generate status reports

launch:
  id: "LAUNCH-2024-AI-CHATBOT-V2"
  system_name: "GenAI Customer Chatbot v2.0"
  target_date: "2024-12-01"
  launch_lead: "kate@corp.com"

task_categories:
  - category: infrastructure
    category_lead: "ivan@corp.com"
    tasks:
      - id: INFRA-001
        title: "Provision GPU node pool for LLM inference"
        dri: ivan@corp.com
        backup_dri: sre-team-lead@corp.com
        status: done          # done | in_progress | not_started | blocked | escalated
        due_date: "2024-11-22"
        notes: "GKE node pool provisioned; auto-scaling config validated"
      - id: INFRA-002
        title: "Configure per-user token rate limits at API gateway"
        dri: ivan@corp.com
        backup_dri: platform-eng@corp.com
        status: in_progress
        due_date: "2024-11-25"
        blocker: null
      - id: INFRA-003
        title: "Validate rollback procedure (test full rollback in staging)"
        dri: ivan@corp.com
        backup_dri: sre-team-lead@corp.com
        status: not_started
        due_date: "2024-11-28"

  - category: ai_specific
    category_lead: grace@corp.com
    tasks:
      - id: AI-001
        title: "Complete model card and obtain legal sign-off"
        dri: grace@corp.com
        backup_dri: henry@corp.com
        status: in_progress
        due_date: "2024-11-20"
        blocker: "Waiting on legal review — escalated to eve@corp.com"
      - id: AI-002
        title: "Validate hallucination guard threshold on evaluation set"
        dri: grace@corp.com
        backup_dri: henry@corp.com
        status: done
        due_date: "2024-11-18"
      - id: AI-003
        title: "Configure and test context-window overflow handling"
        dri: alice@corp.com
        backup_dri: bob@corp.com
        status: blocked
        due_date: "2024-11-22"
        blocker: "SDK bug prevents graceful degradation; ticket raised with provider"

  - category: security
    category_lead: carol@corp.com
    tasks:
      - id: SEC-001
        title: "Complete prompt injection pen test on tool-call endpoints"
        dri: carol@corp.com
        backup_dri: dave@corp.com
        status: in_progress
        due_date: "2024-11-29"
      - id: SEC-002
        title: "Validate output filter blocks PII in LLM responses"
        dri: carol@corp.com
        backup_dri: dave@corp.com
        status: done
        due_date: "2024-11-15"

# task_status_reporter.py — parse launch_tasks.yaml and report status
# pip install pyyaml rich

import yaml
from pathlib import Path
from collections import Counter
from rich.table import Table
from rich.console import Console
from rich.panel import Panel

def load_tasks(yaml_path: str) -> dict:
    return yaml.safe_load(Path(yaml_path).read_text())

def report_launch_status(data: dict) -> None:
    console = Console()
    launch = data["launch"]
    console.print(Panel(
        f"[bold]{launch['system_name']}[/bold]\n"
        f"Target date: {launch['target_date']} | Lead: {launch['launch_lead']}",
        title=f"Launch: {launch['id']}"
    ))

    all_statuses = []
    for category in data["task_categories"]:
        cat_name = category["category"]
        tasks = category["tasks"]
        table = Table(title=f"Category: {cat_name.upper()} (lead: {category['category_lead']})")
        table.add_column("ID", style="cyan", width=10)
        table.add_column("Task", width=45)
        table.add_column("DRI", width=20)
        table.add_column("Status", width=14)
        table.add_column("Due", width=12)

        for t in tasks:
            status = t["status"]
            all_statuses.append(status)
            color = {
                "done": "green", "in_progress": "yellow",
                "not_started": "white", "blocked": "red", "escalated": "magenta"
            }[status]
            blocker_note = f" [red]BLOCKED: {t.get('blocker', '')}[/red]" if status == "blocked" else ""
            table.add_row(
                t["id"],
                t["title"][:45],
                t["dri"].split("@")[0],
                f"[{color}]{status}[/{color}]",
                t["due_date"],
            )
            if status == "blocked":
                console.print(f"  [red]⚠ BLOCKER ({t['id']}): {t.get('blocker', 'unknown')}[/red]")
        console.print(table)

    counts = Counter(all_statuses)
    total = len(all_statuses)
    done = counts.get("done", 0)
    blocked = counts.get("blocked", 0)
    pct = round(100 * done / total) if total else 0
    console.print(f"\n[bold]Overall: {done}/{total} done ({pct}%) | {blocked} blocked[/bold]")
    if blocked > 0:
        console.print("[bold red]LAUNCH READINESS: BLOCKED — resolve blockers before proceeding[/bold red]")
    elif pct >= 100:
        console.print("[bold green]LAUNCH READINESS: READY[/bold green]")
    else:
        console.print(f"[bold yellow]LAUNCH READINESS: IN PROGRESS ({pct}%)[/bold yellow]")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    path = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else "launch_tasks.yaml"
    data = load_tasks(path)
    report_launch_status(data)
Tracking Mechanisms

The choice of tracking mechanism matters less than the discipline of using it. A Google Sheet with strict formatting discipline is more useful than a sophisticated JIRA setup that nobody updates. That said, purpose-built tools have advantages for large launches.
JIRA Epics.

Each launch task becomes a JIRA ticket linked to a launch readiness epic. Assignee = DRI. Status reflects the five states. A dashboard view on the epic shows overall launch readiness at a glance. JIRA automation can notify the launch lead when a ticket transitions to Blocked.
Notion Databases.

A Notion database with properties for task, DRI, category, status, due date, and blocker. Views filtered by status, sorted by due date, allow the launch lead to quickly identify at-risk items. Notion automations can notify DRIs of approaching due dates.
Launch Dashboards.

For large organizations with frequent launches, a custom internal tool that aggregates launch task status across all active launches on a single dashboard is invaluable for portfolio-level visibility. The dashboard shows which launches are at risk (high percentage of blocked or overdue tasks) and enables proactive intervention.
YAML in version control.
Storing the launch task spec as a YAML file (as shown above) gives it version history, enables automated reporting scripts, and allows the task list to be reviewed and updated through pull requests.
Escalation Paths When Owners Are Blocked

A blocked task is one where the DRI cannot make progress without help from outside their immediate scope — a dependency on another team, a tooling issue, an approval that is pending from a third party. The escalation path for a blocked task should be defined before the launch begins, not discovered when the block occurs.
DRI identifies a block and updates the task status to Blocked with a description of the specific blocker in the tracking system.
DRI notifies the launch lead directly (not just by updating the ticket) within the same business day.
Launch lead attempts to unblock within 24 hours by engaging the relevant team directly.

If unblocked within 48 hours is not possible, the launch lead escalates to the launch sponsor (typically a director or VP) with a proposed path forward: extend the deadline, reassign the task, file a PRR exception, or defer the launch.
If the blocker is on the critical path to launch, the go/no-go decision incorporates the blocked task's status.
Real-World Example: Document-Processing AI Agent

An enterprise software company is launching an AI agent that processes uploaded documents, extracts structured data, and populates a database via tool calls. The agent uses an external LLM API for extraction and has tool access to a write endpoint in the company's core database. The launch has 42 tasks across 6 categories.

At the launch kickoff (T-28 days), every task is assigned a DRI. The ML engineer owns all AI-specific tasks (evaluation suite, model card, hallucination guard threshold). The platform engineer owns infrastructure and rate-limit tasks. A security engineer owns security tasks. Product management owns legal coordination and user documentation.

At T-14 days, the weekly launch review surfaces two blocked tasks: (1) the bias audit is blocked because the external auditor is running behind schedule; (2) the rollback test is blocked because the staging environment does not mirror the production database schema correctly. The launch lead escalates both on the same day: the auditor's engagement manager is contacted to accelerate the schedule; the database schema discrepancy is filed as an urgent bug with the platform engineering team.

By T-7 days, 38 of 42 tasks are complete. The remaining four are: bias audit (still in progress, auditor has a preliminary report), rate-limit configuration fine-tuning (owner has an updated config pending validation), AI disclosure text (waiting on legal final approval), and on-call runbook review (waiting for the on-call team to confirm they've read it). None of these are on the critical path to a limited launch. A PRR exception is filed for the incomplete bias audit with a 30-day remediation timeline. The launch proceeds.
Mental Model

Think of launch readiness task ownership as a relay race. In a relay race, the baton is never shared — one runner holds it at any moment, and the handoff is a deliberate, explicit act that both runners are aware of. The race is lost when the baton is dropped between runners because neither one claimed it.

The launch checklist is the race. Each task is a leg. The DRI is the runner currently holding the baton. When the task is complete, the baton is handed to the next person in the chain (the person who will validate or sign off). The launch lead is the race director, watching the entire course, ready to intervene if a runner stumbles. The race cannot finish if any runner is standing at the starting line waiting for a baton that was never explicitly handed to them.
Common Mistakes
Assigning Tasks to Teams Instead of Individuals

"SRE team" is not a DRI. When the owner of a task is a team, every member of the team can assume someone else is handling it. The DRI must be a specific named individual. The team may do the work together, but one person is accountable for reporting status and ensuring completion.
Owners Not Informed Until the Day Before Launch

DRIs who receive their task assignments 24 hours before the launch date have no time to complete meaningful work and no time to escalate blockers. The assignment must happen at least 3–4 weeks before launch for complex tasks and must be accompanied by a clear brief on what "done" looks like for each task.
No Backup DRI for Critical-Path Tasks

A launch that is blocked because the only person who can complete the rollback test is on vacation is a preventable failure. Every task on the critical path must have a backup DRI who is explicitly briefed and who could step in with less than 24 hours' notice.
No Definition of Done

A task with no acceptance criteria creates ambiguity about what "complete" means. "Write the on-call runbook" is incomplete unless it also specifies: "runbook reviewed and confirmed by at least one member of the on-call team who is not the author." Every task should have a crisp definition of what constitutes completion.
Best Practices
One DRI per task — always.
Shared ownership is no ownership. If two people both care about a task, assign one as DRI and the other as a consulted party.
Assign DRIs at the kickoff meeting, in the room.
Ownership assigned synchronously with the person present is more durable than ownership assigned asynchronously via email or a shared document.
Define done for every task.
Include acceptance criteria in the task description so that the DRI and the launch lead have a shared understanding of what completion means.
Balance the load.
After initial assignment, count tasks per DRI. Redistribution may be needed if load is highly uneven.
Weekly status reviews are non-negotiable.
The launch lead must run a weekly review from kickoff to launch. DRIs who miss reviews without submitting a written update should be followed up with directly.
Record the launch task history.
Retain the final state of the launch task list as a snapshot. It becomes the baseline for the post-launch retrospective and the template for the next launch of the same system.
Performance and Scalability
Category leads reduce the cognitive load on the launch lead.

For launches with 50+ tasks, the launch lead cannot effectively track every task individually. Category leads — one per major function — aggregate status and surface blockers to the launch lead, reducing the meeting overhead and the information volume the launch lead must process.
Template reuse across launches.

Each repeated launch of the same system should start from the task list of the previous launch, updating DRIs and due dates rather than re-creating the list from scratch. This dramatically reduces the time to produce the first draft of the task list at the kickoff.
Automated status aggregation.
A script that reads the task YAML and produces a status report (as shown above) reduces the manual effort of compiling launch readiness updates for stakeholders.
Comparison with Alternatives
Ownership Model
Accountability
Escalation clarity
Coordination overhead
Best for
DRI (single owner)
High — one person answerable
High — escalate to named person
Low — no coordination needed
All operational tasks
RACI full model
High — R and A distinct
High — clear escalation chain
Medium — more roles to coordinate
Decision-heavy or sign-off tasks
Team ownership
Low — diffused across team
Low — unclear who to contact
Low — no per-person assignment
Not recommended for launch tasks
No assigned ownership
None
None
None
Actively harmful at launch scale
Tooling and Ecosystem
JIRA.
The assignee field becomes the DRI field. A JIRA Epic dashboard view grouped by status shows launch readiness at a glance. Sprint-based planning can be used to time-box launch preparation work.
Notion.
A launch checklist database with a Person property for DRI, a Select property for status, and a Date property for due date. Gallery views can show task cards grouped by category or sorted by urgency.
Linear.
Issue tracker popular in startup environments; Cycles can be used to group launch tasks into a time-boxed preparation sprint with a natural due date.
GitHub Projects.
For engineering-heavy teams, a GitHub Project board linked to the product repository keeps launch tasks in the same tool as code review, reducing context switching.
Security and Reliability

The task ownership model itself has a reliability consideration: if the tracking system is unavailable, the launch lead must have a fallback for determining task status. Export a snapshot of the launch task list to a shared document at the start of each week so that a tool outage does not create information loss at a critical moment.
Protect the task list from unauthorized modification.

In tracking systems with open edit permissions, a well-intentioned team member can change a task status without authorization. Set edit permissions to DRI-only, with the launch lead having admin access.
Do not delete incomplete tasks.
If a task is deferred or descoped, mark it as deferred with a reason rather than deleting it. The record of what was not completed is part of the launch documentation.
Audit task completions.

In post-launch reviews, verify a sample of tasks that were marked done before the launch. This is not an exercise in distrust but a quality control mechanism that catches the systemic issue of premature completion marking.
Key Takeaways
Every launch checklist item must have a single named DRI — one person whose name is in the owner field and who is accountable for completion or escalation.

AI-specific tasks (model card, hallucination guard, rate-limit configuration, bias audit, context window handling, AI disclosure) sit at team intersections and are most likely to lack ownership without explicit assignment.
Assign DRIs at the launch kickoff meeting, 3–4 weeks before launch; ownership assigned the day before launch is not ownership.
RACI provides a useful elaboration of the DRI model for decision-heavy tasks: distinguish the person doing the work (Responsible) from the person answerable for the outcome (Accountable).
Blocked tasks must be escalated the same day they are identified — a blocked task that sits unescalated is a hidden risk to the launch timeline.
For large launches, use category leads to aggregate task status and reduce the information volume the launch lead must directly manage.
Retain the final state of every launch task list — it is the starting template for the next launch and the basis for launch retrospectives.
